creator: peter.schuller@infidyne.com
text:
For the record, as a user, I would consider setting atime to mtime to be 
unexpected as well.

It is better than setting it to 0 of course, but I would expect the atime to 
be set to the *actual* atime (meaning, the time it was extracted on the local 
system). This does seem to be what gtar is doing (FYI, not trying to invoke 
gtar as the standard).

The motivation for me is that I only see two use cases of atime tar dealing 
with atime. One case is when doing a backup/restore with atime preserved. in 
this case it makes sense to preserve it. But then in this case you should do 
just that - preserve it.

The otherwise typical use of the atime is the actual local access time for 
various purposes (security auditing, tmp cleaning, finding unused large 
garbage, etc).

Barring the backup-and-restore case, I don't see when it is ever useful to set 
the atime to the mtime, while there are clearly cases when it does cause 
problems (e.g. the "grab a tarball and have tmpwatch/tmpreaper kill it in an 
hour").

(My use of ">= creation time" (modification time) in the original bug report 
was mostly meant to suggest that setting it to < creation time (modification 
time) makes no sense at all unless preserving.)

-- 
/ Peter Schuller
